```{r block26}
mean_cottonwood_surv <- mean(cottonwood_surv_base)

first_sens_run_summary$cottonwood_surv <- first_sens_run_summary$offset + mean_cottonwood_surv

first_sens_run_summary_ht <- subset(first_sens_run_summary, projection < 6)
first_sens_run_summary_ht_1 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_ht, mpm == 1)
first_sens_run_summary_ht_2 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_ht, mpm == 2)
first_sens_run_summary_ht_3 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_ht, mpm == 3)
first_sens_run_summary_ht_4 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_ht, mpm == 4)
first_sens_run_summary_ht_5 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_ht, mpm == 5)

first_sens_ht_surv_1 <- lm(final_N ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_ht_1)
first_sens_ht_surv_2 <- lm(final_N ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_ht_2)
first_sens_ht_surv_3 <- lm(final_N ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_ht_3)
first_sens_ht_surv_4 <- lm(final_N ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_ht_4)
first_sens_ht_surv_5 <- lm(final_N ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_ht_5)

summary(first_sens_ht_surv_1)
summary(first_sens_ht_surv_2)
summary(first_sens_ht_surv_3)
summary(first_sens_ht_surv_4)
summary(first_sens_ht_surv_5)
```
  
  The sensitivity of each final population size to changes in the survival of the oldest cottonwoods is given by the slopes of these linear regressions. However, in all cases, these slopes do not differ significantly from 0. Let's take a look at a plot of the impacts of changes in survival to the final population size of cottonwoods.
```{r block27, fig.cap = "Final population size of cottonwood as a function of additive offset to cottonwood oldest adult survival."}
plot(final_N ~ offset, first_sens_run_summary_ht_1, ylab = "Final population size",
  xlab = "Additive Offset to Oldest Cottonwood Survival", bty = "n")
abline(first_sens_ht_surv_1)
```
  
  We can also look at the impact of changes in survival in the oldest cottonwoods to the final population size of tamarisk, as below.
```{r block28, fig.cap = "Final population size of tamarisk as a function of additive offset to cottonwood oldest adult survival."}
plot(final_N ~ offset, first_sens_run_summary_ht_2, ylab = "Final population size",
  xlab = "Additive Offset to Oldest Cottonwood Survival", bty = "n")
abline(first_sens_ht_surv_2)
```
  
  Of course, the final population size in a stochastic projection, or in any projection, may be too variable to make proper inferences from. We may wish, instead, to use the mean population size for some number of time steps toward the end of the projection. Fortunately, the `summary.adaptProjBatch()` function comes to our aid in this with the `finalN_mean` and `finalN_used` arguments, where the former specifies that we would like the mean of the number of final time steps specified by the latter. The default setting for the former is `FALSE`, and the default setting for the latter is `100`. Let's alter both and re-assess the sensitivities.
```{r block29}
first_sens_run_summary_means <- summary(first_sens_run, finalN_mean = TRUE, finalN_used = 50)
first_sens_run_summary_means$cottonwood_surv <- first_sens_run_summary_means$offset + mean_cottonwood_surv

first_sens_run_summary_means_ht <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_means, projection < 6)
first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_1 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_means_ht, mpm == 1)
first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_2 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_means_ht, mpm == 2)
first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_3 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_means_ht, mpm == 3)
first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_4 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_means_ht, mpm == 4)
first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_5 <- subset(first_sens_run_summary_means_ht, mpm == 5)

first_sens_ht_means_surv_1 <- lm(final_N_mean ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_1)
first_sens_ht_means_surv_2 <- lm(final_N_mean ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_2)
first_sens_ht_means_surv_3 <- lm(final_N_mean ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_3)
first_sens_ht_means_surv_4 <- lm(final_N_mean ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_4)
first_sens_ht_means_surv_5 <- lm(final_N_mean ~ cottonwood_surv, first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_5)

summary(first_sens_ht_means_surv_1)
summary(first_sens_ht_means_surv_2)
summary(first_sens_ht_means_surv_3)
summary(first_sens_ht_means_surv_4)
summary(first_sens_ht_means_surv_5)
```
  
  It appears that the survival of the oldest cottonwoods does indeed have an influence on this community, particularly through impacts on tamarisks, willows, and meadow plants. Let's take a look at a plot for tamarisks.
```{r block30, fig.cap = "Mean final population size of tamarisk as a function of additive offset to cottonwood oldest adult survival."}
plot(final_N ~ offset, first_sens_run_summary_means_ht_2, ylab = "Mean final population size",
  xlab = "Additive Offset to Cottonwood Survival", bty = "n")
abline(first_sens_ht_means_surv_2)
```  
  
  For more complex sensitivities, users will need to go into the list output by function `batch_project3()`.
  
  In addition to standard matrix element sensitivities, users may wish to conduct "lower-level" sensitivities. The MCM from @lytle_linking_2017 is a perfect example to learn from, because the matrices packaged into the MCM are all developed from a series of functions in which matrix elements are actually functions of a series of demographic and environmental variables. Indeed, the authors conducted such a sensitivity analysis by focusing on the impacts of changes to what they termed the "flood mortality in a flood year" (S), the "drought mortality in a drought year", and the "baseline transition probability". We will not conduct all of these analyses, but will instead focus just on determining the sensitivities of mean final population size of our plant guilds to changes in the drought mortality associated with cottonwoods.
  
  In the block below, we create five new sets of MCMs, using the `matrix_creator()` function that we developed earlier. In each case, we alter drought mortality associated with cottonwoods by a small, additive offset.
```{r block31}
riparian_mats_ht_drought_1 <- matrix_creator(flowdata, total_years,
  DC6_offset = 0.005)
riparian_mats_ht_drought_2 <- matrix_creator(flowdata, total_years,
  DC6_offset = 0.010)
riparian_mats_ht_drought_3 <- matrix_creator(flowdata, total_years,
  DC6_offset = 0.015)
riparian_mats_ht_drought_4 <- matrix_creator(flowdata, total_years,
  DC6_offset = 0.020)
riparian_mats_ht_drought_5 <- matrix_creator(flowdata, total_years,
  DC6_offset = 0.025)

cottonwood_lM_1 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_1$cottonwood, tree_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
tamarisk_lM_1 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_1$tamarisk, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
sagebrush_lM_1 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_1$sagebrush, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
meadow_lM_1 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_1$meadow, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
willow_lM_1 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_1$willow, will_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))

cottonwood_lM_2 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_2$cottonwood, tree_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
tamarisk_lM_2 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_2$tamarisk, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
sagebrush_lM_2 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_2$sagebrush, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
meadow_lM_2 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_2$meadow, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
willow_lM_2 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_2$willow, will_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))

cottonwood_lM_3 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_3$cottonwood, tree_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
tamarisk_lM_3 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_3$tamarisk, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
sagebrush_lM_3 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_3$sagebrush, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
meadow_lM_3 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_3$meadow, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
willow_lM_3 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_3$willow, will_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))

cottonwood_lM_4 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_4$cottonwood, tree_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
tamarisk_lM_4 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_4$tamarisk, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
sagebrush_lM_4 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_4$sagebrush, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
meadow_lM_4 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_4$meadow, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
willow_lM_4 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_4$willow, will_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))

cottonwood_lM_5 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_5$cottonwood, tree_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
tamarisk_lM_5 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_5$tamarisk, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
sagebrush_lM_5 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_5$sagebrush, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
meadow_lM_5 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_5$meadow, mead_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))
willow_lM_5 <- create_lM(riparian_mats_ht_drought_5$willow, will_frame,
  yearorder = c(1:83))

lytle_mpms_1 <- list(cottonwood_lM_1, tamarisk_lM_1, willow_lM_1,
  sagebrush_lM_1, meadow_lM_1)
lytle_mpms_2 <- list(cottonwood_lM_2, tamarisk_lM_2, willow_lM_2,
  sagebrush_lM_2, meadow_lM_2)
lytle_mpms_3 <- list(cottonwood_lM_3, tamarisk_lM_3, willow_lM_3,
  sagebrush_lM_3, meadow_lM_3)
lytle_mpms_4 <- list(cottonwood_lM_4, tamarisk_lM_4, willow_lM_4,
  sagebrush_lM_4, meadow_lM_4)
lytle_mpms_5 <- list(cottonwood_lM_5, tamarisk_lM_5, willow_lM_5,
  sagebrush_lM_5, meadow_lM_5)
```
  
  Now we will run five separate projections, each with a series of replicates.
```{r block32}
Dsens_proj_1 <- project3(mpms = lytle_mpms_1, times = times_projected,
    nreps = sens_reps, equivalence = lytle_alleq, density = lytle_alldens,
    starts = lytle_starts, stochastic = TRUE)
Dsens_proj_2 <- project3(mpms = lytle_mpms_2, times = times_projected,
    nreps = sens_reps, equivalence = lytle_alleq, density = lytle_alldens,
    starts = lytle_starts, stochastic = TRUE)
Dsens_proj_3 <- project3(mpms = lytle_mpms_3, times = times_projected,
    nreps = sens_reps, equivalence = lytle_alleq, density = lytle_alldens,
    starts = lytle_starts, stochastic = TRUE)
Dsens_proj_4 <- project3(mpms = lytle_mpms_4, times = times_projected,
    nreps = sens_reps, equivalence = lytle_alleq, density = lytle_alldens,
    starts = lytle_starts, stochastic = TRUE)
Dsens_proj_5 <- project3(mpms = lytle_mpms_5, times = times_projected,
    nreps = sens_reps, equivalence = lytle_alleq, density = lytle_alldens,
    starts = lytle_starts, stochastic = TRUE)
```
  
  The function `summary.adaptProj()` can help us make sense of the output. As before, we can extract both the final population size of each guild, as well as the mean final population size. Let's do that, as below.
```{r block33}
summary(Dsens_proj_1, finalN_mean = TRUE, finalN_used = 50)
summary(Dsens_proj_2, finalN_mean = TRUE, finalN_used = 50)
summary(Dsens_proj_3, finalN_mean = TRUE, finalN_used = 50)
summary(Dsens_proj_4, finalN_mean = TRUE, finalN_used = 50)
summary(Dsens_proj_5, finalN_mean = TRUE, finalN_used = 50)
```
  
  Although the summaries are certainly interesting, they show the mean final population size not simply as the mean of the final 50 time steps here, but also across all of the replicates. We would naturally like to extract that data in order to run linear regressions of the mean final population size against the offset. Below, we extract those data, and also develop the necessary data frames for further analysis.
```{r block34}
Dsens_proj_1_finalNmeans <- as.vector(apply(as.matrix(c(1:sens_reps)), 1, function(X) {
  apply(Dsens_proj_1$N_out[[X]][,c(151:201)], 1, mean)
}))
Dsens_proj_2_finalNmeans <- as.vector(apply(as.matrix(c(1:sens_reps)), 1, function(X) {
  apply(Dsens_proj_2$N_out[[X]][,c(151:201)], 1, mean)
}))
Dsens_proj_3_finalNmeans <- as.vector(apply(as.matrix(c(1:sens_reps)), 1, function(X) {
  apply(Dsens_proj_3$N_out[[X]][,c(151:201)], 1, mean)
}))
Dsens_proj_4_finalNmeans <- as.vector(apply(as.matrix(c(1:sens_reps)), 1, function(X) {
  apply(Dsens_proj_4$N_out[[X]][,c(151:201)], 1, mean)
}))
Dsens_proj_5_finalNmeans <- as.vector(apply(as.matrix(c(1:sens_reps)), 1, function(X) {
  apply(Dsens_proj_5$N_out[[X]][,c(151:201)], 1, mean)
}))

Dsens_proj_mpm_ids <- rep(seq(from = 1, to = 5), sens_reps)

Dsens_proj_1_df <- data.frame(proj = 1, offset = 0.005, mpm = Dsens_proj_mpm_ids,
  finalN_mean = Dsens_proj_1_finalNmeans)
Dsens_proj_2_df <- data.frame(proj = 1, offset = 0.010, mpm = Dsens_proj_mpm_ids,
  finalN_mean = Dsens_proj_2_finalNmeans)
Dsens_proj_3_df <- data.frame(proj = 1, offset = 0.015, mpm = Dsens_proj_mpm_ids,
  finalN_mean = Dsens_proj_3_finalNmeans)
Dsens_proj_4_df <- data.frame(proj = 1, offset = 0.020, mpm = Dsens_proj_mpm_ids,
  finalN_mean = Dsens_proj_4_finalNmeans)
Dsens_proj_5_df <- data.frame(proj = 1, offset = 0.025, mpm = Dsens_proj_mpm_ids,
  finalN_mean = Dsens_proj_5_finalNmeans)

Dsens_proj_df <- rbind.data.frame(Dsens_proj_1_df, Dsens_proj_2_df,
  Dsens_proj_3_df, Dsens_proj_4_df, Dsens_proj_5_df)
Dsens_proj_df$drought_mortality <- Dsens_proj_df$offset + 0.05 # This term comes from term DC6 in the matrix_creator() function
```
  
  We now have a data frame assessing the result. Let's now conduct the linear regressions of the mean final population size against the offset, by guild.
```{r block35}
Dsens_proj_df_cottonwood <- subset(Dsens_proj_df, mpm == 1)
Dsens_proj_df_tamarisk <- subset(Dsens_proj_df, mpm == 2)
Dsens_proj_df_willow <- subset(Dsens_proj_df, mpm == 3)
Dsens_proj_df_sagebrush <- subset(Dsens_proj_df, mpm == 4)
Dsens_proj_df_meadow <- subset(Dsens_proj_df, mpm == 5)

Dsens_prof_df_cottonwood_sens <- lm(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_cottonwood)
Dsens_prof_df_tamarisk_sens <- lm(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_tamarisk)
Dsens_prof_df_willow_sens <- lm(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_willow)
Dsens_prof_df_sagebrush_sens <- lm(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_sagebrush)
Dsens_prof_df_meadow_sens <- lm(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_meadow)

summary(Dsens_prof_df_cottonwood_sens)
summary(Dsens_prof_df_tamarisk_sens)
summary(Dsens_prof_df_willow_sens)
summary(Dsens_prof_df_sagebrush_sens)
summary(Dsens_prof_df_meadow_sens)
```
  
  The results suggest significant influences of changes to the drought mortality in cottonwoods on the mean final population sizes of willows (hydroriparian shrubs) and meadow plants. Let's take plot these results, first for willows.
```{r block36, fig.cap = "Mean final population size of willow as a function of cottonwood drought mortality."}
plot(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_willow, ylab = "Mean final population size",
  xlab = "Cottonwood Drought Mortality", bty = "n")
abline(Dsens_prof_df_willow_sens)
```
  
  We see a significant negative influence in the plot, meaning that as cottonwood drought mortality increases, the final population size of willows decreases. Let's now also take a look at the impact on meadow plants.
```{r block37, fig.cap = "Mean final population size of meadow plants as a function of cottonwood drought mortality."}
plot(finalN_mean ~ drought_mortality, Dsens_proj_df_meadow, ylab = "Mean final population size",
  xlab = "Cottonwood Drought Mortality", bty = "n")
abline(Dsens_prof_df_meadow_sens)
```
  
  Once again, we see a negative impact. If we take the slopes of these regressions to be equal to the sensitivity of the mean final population size to cottonwood drought mortality, then these sensitivity terms are equal to the following.
```{r block38}
Dsens_prof_df_willow_sens$coefficients
```
  
  The sensitivity protocols, batch projection functions, and other aspects of package `adapt3` should be of interest to population and community ecologists interested in working further with MPMs. We will continue to add further functionality in this package with time.
  
  
Literature Cited
